text.title: match-real-world Failure Modes
text.summary: Migrated antipatterns for match-real-world as failure modes. Add detector + repair clauses where missing.

failure-mode.description: Technical jargon as primary UI copy: "Configure SMTP parameters for outbound mail relay"
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Requires technical knowledge the user does not have. Rewrite: "Set up email sending". The action is the same; the user does not need to know what SMTP is.

failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Confirms the system's action, not the user's goal. Rewrite: "Your booking is confirmed" — confirms what the user was trying to achieve.

failure-mode.description: Inconsistent terminology across pages: "Sign in" on homepage, "Login" on mobile, "Log in" in email
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Forces users to question whether these are the same action. Pick one term and apply it everywhere. Inconsistency is a heuristic #2 violation regardless of individual clarity.

failure-mode.description: Locale-blind date format: displaying "03/04/2025" without locale detection
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: March 4 in US format is April 3 in European format. For financial, travel, and medical products, this is not just confusing — it can cause errors with real consequences. Use unambiguous formats or locale detection.

failure-mode.description: Forced simplification of technical terms that users expect: changing "invoice" to "bill" in an accounting tool
failure-mode.detector: TBD — observable signal that this antipattern is happening
failure-mode.repair: Domain-expert users have precise vocabulary expectations. Over-simplifying removes signal. For expert-user products, "plain language" means the domain's language, not generic consumer language.
